summary: Drew DeVault's October 2019 status update covers progress across his many free software projects. The centerpiece is SourceHut's evolution toward integrating its services, particularly enabling email-backed code review through git.sr.ht and lists.sr.ht interoperability. He announces Simon Ser joining SourceHut to work on self-directed free software projects, funded by SourceHut's profits. The update also covers sway/wlroots improvements targeting 1ms input latency, contributions to the Monado OpenXR runtime, progress on the Wayland book, and various smaller projects.
key_points: SourceHut is entering its third phase: teaching its services to talk to each other, enabling web-based code review backed by email
SourceHut will spend profits sponsoring free software developers, with Simon Ser as the first hire for self-directed open source work
Sway's next release targets 1ms input latency, VNC support via screencopy, and drawing tablet support
DeVault is contributing to Monado (the open-source OpenXR runtime), overhauling its build system and Wayland backend
A second POWER9 machine is being set up for testing Wayland and Mesa on ppc64le architecture
money_quotes: Sourcehut's development plans can be described in three broad strokes: (1) make a bunch of services (or: primitives for a development hub); (2) rig them all up with APIs and webhooks; and (3) teach them how to talk to each other.
This meets an original design goal of Sourcehut: to build user-friendly tools on top of existing systems.
There's also a fourth stage of Sourcehut: giving back to the free software community.
To this end, I intend to spend Sourcehut's profits on sponsoring motivated and talented free software developers to work on self-directed projects.
We expect to ship patches which can reduce input latency to as low as 1ms.
We're also making strong headway on a long-term project to overhaul rendering and DRM in wlroots, with the long term goal of achieving the holy grail levels of performance on any device.
key_insight: SourceHut is maturing from a collection of independent services into an integrated development platform, while also beginning to fulfill its mission of financially sustaining free software developers.
